content: Microscopy and Microanalysis, 29 (Suppl 1), 2023, 111–112 https://doi.org/10.1093/micmic/ozad067.048

paper_title: EELS Characterization of Niobium Oxide Memristor Devices, Microanal
content:
1US Naval Research Laboratory, Washington, DC, USA

*Corresponding author: bradley.degregorio@nrl.navy.mil

Volatile memristor devices are used in neuromorphic computing systems because they mimic the spike action of neurons in the brain [1]. A physical artificial neural network can be constructed using threshold (i.e., rapid) switching phase change materials such as TiO2 or NbO2 to meet the non-linear and “short term memory” requirements for a reservoir computing architecture. In the case of NbO2, an induced current applied to the device causes localized Joule heating to enable a reversible i

paper_title: EELS Characterization of Niobium Oxide Memristor Devices, Microanal
content: Characterization of the unheated sample reveals that three compositions of NbOx were deposited in a multilayer film by plasma-assisted ALD (Figure 1). The first layer (30 nm) at the bottom of the stack is crystalline and shows O-K and Nb-N spectra that match R-NbO2. Above this layer is 30 nm of amorphous film, where the O-K spectra of top 15 nm is consistent with the predominantly corner-sharing NbO6 octahedra of Nb2O5 phases. The O-K spectrum of the middle 15 nm layer does not match either of the two reference spectra, and the broad peak at 540 eV indicates that complex O coordination is pres

paper_title: Pure CMOS embedded Artificial Synaptic Device (eASD) for High Density Neuromorph
content:
Hsin-Yuan Yu[1] , Yao-Hung Huang[1] , Yue-Der Chih[2] , Jonathan Chang[2] , Ya-Chin King[1] , Chrong Jung Lin[1]

1 Institute of Electronics Engineering, National Tsing Hua University (NTHU), Hsinchu, Taiwan

2 Design Technology Platform (DTP), Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company (TSMC), Hsinchu, Taiwan E-mail: cjlin@ee.nthu.edu.tw

_**Abstract**_ **— An embedded Artificial Synaptic Device (eASD) featuring high-performance and high-density characteristics with n-MOSFET’s high-k layer as memristive switching pairs in pure 28nm CMOS process is firstly proposed. Taking the advantages of re

paper_title: Pure CMOS embedded Artificial Synaptic Device (eASD) for High Density Neuromorph
content: RRAM device featuring CMOS-technology-process compatibility and high density is a promising candidate for realizing Neural Network (NN) [1], Computing In Memory (CIM)[2] and Neuromorphic systems[3]. In this work, we newly investigate an embedded Non-Volatile Latch Logic structure by TSMC’s pure CMOS Logic 28nm HKMG process to realize a pair of complimentary memristors in n- MOSFET atomic layer deposition (ALD) of high-k gate dielectric layer. eASD can further be shrank to be an ultrahigh density one-transistor eASD structure **(HD eASD)** by utilizing the high-k gate dielectric layer overlappi

paper_title: Pure CMOS embedded Artificial Synaptic Device (eASD) for High Density Neuromorph
content: The 3D structure, layout illustration and circuit schematic of the high performance **e** mbedded **A** rtificial **S** ynaptic **D** evice **(eASD)** are shown in **Fig.1(a)** The cross-sectional transmission electron microscopy (TEM) pictures and EDX of materials are shown in **Fig.1(b)** . The new non-volatile latch consists of one 28nm HKMG n-channel MOSFET as a select transistor and two cascaded n-MOSFETs as a high density memorized unit. Besides, two memristors **(MR1 & MR2)** of high performance eASD are independently formed at the drain sides of the two serial memory transistors. In th

paper_title: Pure CMOS embedded Artificial Synaptic Device (eASD) for High Density Neuromorph
content: The operation steps and characteristics are demonstrated in **Fig.2** . By applying large voltage on **D1** and **D2** terminals, both memristors are initially “Set” to Low Memristance (LMR). Upon writing “1”, MR1 is then “Reset” to High Memristance (HMR) while MR2 still keeps at LMR. The same reset operation is performed on MR2 to complete writing “0” with MR1 keeping at LMR. While the select transistor is off at “Read” step, a complementary state formed by the opposite state of MR1 and MR2 can efficiently selfdetermine the output signal, resulting in the corresponding voltage ( **VY** ) , wh

paper_title: Pure CMOS embedded Artificial Synaptic Device (eASD) for High Density Neuromorph
content: A challenge of achieving high density eASD is the unit size. By using both source and drain sides of the HKMG transistor’s n+ SiP junctions, eASD can reach double latched data density in one transistor. **Fig.3** shows the 3D structure, schematic, and layout of the High Density eASD **(HD eASD)** . As the device scaling down, the concern of MR1 and MR2 asymmetry may raise up. However, the distribution of **Fig.4** shows no degradation of memristance window in HD eASD array. The operation conditions for HP and HD eASDs are summarized in **Fig.5** .

paper_title: Pure CMOS embedded Artificial Synaptic Device (eASD) for High Density Neuromorph
content:
The eASD neuromorphic system depicted in **Fig.6** is applying the high density eASD **(HD eASD)** to an array and placing a diode between each output terminal of eASD for rectification. The switch states of the diode are determined and related with the output voltages of adjacent eASDs. The current of each row is summed to the output end **(Yn)** . **Table 1** shows the correlated bias condition of eASD and the restored synaptic state processes.

While processing in the synaptic state, the different neuron weighted current can be produced by assigning SL voltages. In order to analyze On/Off f

paper_title: Pure CMOS embedded Artificial Synaptic Device (eASD) for High Density Neuromorph
content:
In this work, an embedded artificial synaptic devices ( **eASD** ) fully compatible to pure CMOS HKMG logic process is firstly proposed, showing advantages in neuromorphic computing systems. Both high performance and high density eASD feature high data transmission speed, stability characteristics of output with high writing efficiency and stable read window.

REFERENCES

- [1] [1] C. Nebauer, in IEEE Transactions on Neural Networks, vol. 9, no. 4, pp. 685-696, July 1998

- [2] Peng Yao et al, IEEE, IEEE 11th International Memory Workshop (IMW), 2019

- [3] T. C. Jackson et al, 2015 IEEE, LASC

paper_title: Dopant Control by Atomic Layer Deposition in Oxide Films for Memristive Switches
content: Among the variety of fabrication techniques, atomic layer deposition (ALD)[25] is likely the most technologically relevant and ALD oxide films have been extensively studied as gate oxides in the metal-oxide-semiconductor field-effect transistor (MOSFET) structure and dielectric media in the DRAM stack capacitors. The focus has been on reducing the leakage current as well as increasing the dielectric constant and the breakdown voltage in the previous work,[25,26] where stoichiometric oxides were desired. However, for memristor applications, the composition of the oxides needs to be tuned within

paper_title: Foldable neuromorphic memristive electronics
content:
Mohamed T. Ghoneim, _Student Member, IEEE_ , Mohammed A. Zidan, _Student Member, IEEE,_ Khaled. N. Salama, _Senior Member, IEEE_ and Muhammad M. Hussain, _Senior Member, IEEE_

_**Abstract**_ **—Neuromorphic computer will need folded architectural form factor to match brain cortex’s folded pattern for ultra-compact design. In this work, we show a state-of-theart CMOS compatible pragmatic fabrication approach of building structurally foldable and densely integrated neuromorphic devices for non-volatile memory applications. We report the first ever memristive devices with the size of a motor neu

paper_title: Foldable neuromorphic memristive electronics
content: SINCEexperimental  their conceptualization and then with their demonstration, memristive devices (popularly memristors) have attracted lots of interest. This is because they offer an alternative to complementary metal oxide semiconductor (CMOS) based flash non-volatile memory (NVM) which is approaching scaling limits. For instance, International Technology Roadmap for Semiconductors (ITRS) report of 2012 indicates flash memory ½ pitch should reach 10 nm by 2020 [1]. Cross bars resistive RAMs with 10 nm x 10 nm cell size have already been reported [2]. Additionally it can also be a complementar

paper_title: Foldable neuromorphic memristive electronics
content: We utilized a 300 nm thermally grown SiO2 on Si (100) 4’’ wafers for devices isolation, Figure 1, a. The oxide layer is then patterned using photolithographic techniques to have 10 µm diameter holes with 20 µm pitch, followed by a deep reactive ion etching (DRIE) down to 25 µm depth, figure 1, b. Then a spacer formation process is carried out using conformal atomic layer deposition (ALD) of 50 nm Al2O3 at 300°C and a consecutive directional reactive ion etch (RIE) that removes the spacer from vertical surfaces leaving the sidewalls protection intact (not shown). Finally, the sample is placed i

content:

Si takes place at the bottom of the deep hole network to peel off a thin Si (100) fabric that is flexible up to a bending radius of 10 mm (0.125% nominal strain, ɛnominal= _t_ /2 _R_ , where _t_ is the substrate thickness and _R_ is the bending radius).

In our process we released a 3 x 4 cm[2] area of the 4’’ wafer for flexible memristors while the rest hosted the bulk devices for a comparative analysis. The devices were built using 200 nm sputtered Al commo

paper_title: Foldable neuromorphic memristive electronics
content: Owing to the variability control in this experiment as foldable and bulk devices were both fabricated in the same process (one 4’’ wafer having 12 cm[2] flexible fabric area and the rest is bulk), we can fairly relate observed differences to the prior release step. Basic IV characterization of 10 flexible/foldable (transformed) and 10 bulk/inflexible (traditional) devices were performed on Keithley 4200 SCS. Figure 3, a) shows the hysteresis properties of the average values for bulk and foldable devices. The results indicate that there is no significant deviation in device properties due to th

paper_title: Foldable neuromorphic memristive electronics
content: We have reported foldable memristors fabricated on bulk mono-crystalline silicon (100) using standard CMOS processes. The results show that the basic memristive IV properties of these devices match that of their bulk counterparts with no significant deviation. We believe our process offers a pragmatic step towards densely integrated memristive systems, structurally capable of mimicking biological neuromorphic systems.
